blocks.text: If $z$ is a function of $x$ and $y$, i.e., $z = f(x, y)$, the total differential $dz$ is obtained from the partial differentials $dx$ and $dy$ by the use of the following formula:

blocks.description: This formula expresses how the total change in $z$ can be accounted for by changes in $x$ and $y$.

blocks.text: The reason for this is more clearly seen if we work from the fundamental idea of $x$ and $y$, and introduce the actually measurable quantities like $x$, $y$, and $z$.

blocks.description: The figure illustrates the relationship between points in a plane and how the change in one variable affects another, emphasizing the significance of rates of change.

blocks.latex: \text{total change in } z = \text{ change in } z \text{ due to the change in } x.
blocks.description: This equation summarizes the change in $z$ as influenced by changes in $x$.

blocks.text: Let $P$ be a point $(x, y)$ on a surface, and let $P$ move to a new position $Q$ near to $P$. The change of position is most easily referred to by a diagram.
